SB1086.title: Zoning: allowed uses: microschools and micro-education entities.
SB1086.author: Megan Dahle
SB1086.summary: An act to add Section 65852.10 to the Government Code, relating to land use.

actions.description: Introduced. Read first time. To Com. on RLS. for assignment. To print.
From printer. May be acted upon on or after March 16.

analysis.notes: SB 1086 adds Government Code §65852.10, which would require cities and counties to allow microschools and micro-education entities as permitted uses in zoning districts — overriding local land use decisions about where educational uses are appropriate. This constitutes a partial preemption of local zoning authority (Criterion A), likely mandates ministerial or by-right approval for qualifying micro-education uses (Criterion B/C), and shifts permitting and compliance administration costs to local governments without evident state funding (Criterion D).
analysis.comms_brief:
SB 1086 adds Government Code §65852.10 to require cities and counties to permit microschools and micro-education entities as allowed uses in local zoning, overriding local land use authority to determine where educational uses are appropriate.
• Preempts local zoning authority by mandating that microschools be treated as allowed uses regardless of a city's or county's general plan and zoning code designations.
• Likely eliminates or severely restricts local discretionary review — conditional use permits, design review, and neighborhood compatibility hearings — for qualifying micro-education facilities.
• Forces cities to approve a new category of institutional use that communities have not chosen to permit, effectively mandating development decisions beyond local preference.
• Shifts administrative and compliance costs onto local planning departments without identified state funding to offset the new permitting and enforcement obligations.
Recommended: Oppose — the bill explicitly overrides local zoning authority across all district types with no local opt-out or mitigation mechanism, and strips discretionary review for a new institutional use category cities have not chosen to permit.

SB1003.title: Prohousing enhanced infrastructure financing districts.
SB1003.author: Tim Grayson
SB1003.summary: An act to amend Section 65589.9 of, and to add Article 5 (commencing with Section 53398.89) to Chapter 2.99 of Part 1 of Division 2 of Title 5 of, the Government Code, relating to land use.

actions.description: From committee: Do pass as amended and re-refer to Com. on APPR. (Ayes 8. Noes 2.) (April 21).

Read second time and amended. Ordered to second reading.
Read second time. Ordered to third reading.
Read third time. Passed. (Ayes 32. Noes 7.) Ordered to the Assembly.
In Assembly. Read first time. Held at Desk.

analysis.notes: SB 1003 creates a new "Prohousing Enhanced Infrastructure Financing District" (PEIFD) vehicle that links EIFD formation and tax-increment financing to prohousing designation status, creating fiscal incentives that steer local land use decisions toward state-preferred housing policies and eroding local autonomy over planning priorities. By tying enhanced financing tools to prohousing compliance (amended Section 65589.9), the bill creates structured pressure on cities to adopt state-aligned zoning and land use standards — a moderate local control override and an effective mandate — while the tax-increment mechanism may divert revenues cities would otherwise direct to infrastructure and services, shifting capacity costs indirectly onto existing residents.
analysis.comms_brief:
SB 1003 creates a new class of Enhanced Infrastructure Financing Districts available only to cities that achieve a state "Prohousing" designation, conditioning access to tax-increment financing tools on compliance with state housing policy preferences and effectively coercing local land use decisions.
• Conditions a significant local financing tool on state-defined prohousing compliance, pressuring cities to adopt zoning and planning policies they may not otherwise choose.
• Tax-increment diversion through PEIFDs can redirect revenues away from general city services and existing infrastructure obligations, shifting fiscal burden to current residents.
• Linking EIFD authority to prohousing status (via amended Section 65589.9) creates a state-directed incentive structure that overrides local deliberation on how to prioritize housing policy.
Recommended: Monitor with caution — the bill's coercive incentive structure is a meaningful indirect-to-moderate local control risk, and amendments in committee may sharpen or soften the compliance conditions; cities should assess how prohousing designation requirements interact with their existing general plans.

analysis.model: claude-sonnet-4-6 (via Claude Code) — D criterion reanalysis
analysis.comms_brief:
AB1751 (Missing Middle Townhome Ownership Act) creates a statewide ministerial pathway for townhome development on any residential parcel, overriding local single-family zoning without community input.
• Cities lose all discretionary authority — projects meeting state objective standards must be approved regardless of neighborhood character, infrastructure capacity, or local planning
• Eliminates design review and public hearings for qualifying developments; no CEQA review applies
• No impact fee authority: cities cannot charge water, sewer, traffic, or school impact fees for new townhome residents — infrastructure costs shift entirely to existing taxpayers
• Sets a precedent for further ministerial preemption of residential zoning across the state
Recommended: CSF should actively oppose; submit formal comment letters and coordinate with planning directors to document infrastructure and service capacity concerns.

SB1014.author: Tim Grayson
SB1014.summary: An act to add Section 65941.3 to the Government Code, relating to development projects.

In Assembly. Read first time. Held at Desk.

analysis.notes: SB 1014 requires local agencies to provide applicants with a preliminary estimate of required onsite and offsite improvements early in the development review process (new Gov. Code § 65941.3). While framed as a transparency measure, this creates a procedural obligation that constrains local discretion by locking in improvement expectations at an early, pre-entitlement stage — potentially limiting cities' ability to later impose additional infrastructure or mitigation conditions as project details evolve. The bill also imposes an unfunded compliance burden on local planning departments to produce timely, formal improvement estimates, which may require additional staffing or systems without state funding offset.
analysis.comms_brief:
SB 1014 requires cities and counties to provide development applicants with a formal preliminary estimate of required onsite and offsite improvements early in the entitlement process, creating procedural obligations that constrain local flexibility on infrastructure conditioning.
• Early disclosure of improvement estimates could be used by applicants to legally challenge or resist conditions imposed later in the review process, effectively capping cities' ability to require additional mitigation as project scope becomes clearer.
• The unfunded mandate to produce timely, formal improvement estimates imposes new staffing and administrative costs on local planning departments with no identified state funding offset.
• Locking in improvement expectations at a pre-entitlement stage undermines the iterative nature of local development review, where infrastructure needs are often refined through CEQA analysis, engineering review, and public hearings.
Recommended: Monitor — the bill's risk is moderate and procedural rather than an outright preemption, but the potential for improvement estimates to be weaponized against later conditions warrants close attention to Assembly amendments.

AB1997.title: Land use: housing development approvals: timelines and processes.
AB1997.author: Alex Lee
AB1997.summary: An act to amend Sections 65589.5, 65589.9, and 65950 of, and to add Section 65943.3 to, the Government Code, and to add Section 50411 to the Health and Safety Code, relating to housing.

actions.description: Read first time. To print.
From printer. May be heard in committee March 20.

analysis.notes: AB 1997 amends Housing Accountability Act provisions (§65589.5, §65589.9) and approval timeline statutes (§65950), and adds new streamlining mandates (§65943.3, HSC §50411). Taken together, these amendments tighten state control over local approval timelines, limit cities' ability to deny or condition housing projects under the HAA, and impose compliance requirements on local agencies — all of which restrict local discretion and shift infrastructure review authority away from cities without clear funding offsets.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 1997 amends the Housing Accountability Act and local approval timeline statutes to restrict cities' ability to deny housing projects, compress permitting timelines, and impose new compliance mandates on local agencies.
• Amendments to §65589.5 (HAA) further limit the findings cities may use to deny or condition housing projects, narrowing local discretion over project approval.
• Changes to §65950 compress or restructure local approval timelines, reducing cities' capacity to conduct thorough infrastructure, design, and community review before project approval.
• Addition of §65943.3 and HSC §50411 creates new streamlining mandates and compliance obligations on local agencies with no clear state funding offset for staffing or administrative costs.
• Tightened HAA enforcement under §65589.9 increases litigation exposure for cities that attempt to apply objective local standards or deny projects on capacity grounds.
Recommended: Oppose — bill layers multiple HAA and timeline restrictions that cumulatively strip local discretion over project conditions, timelines, and denial authority with no mechanism to replace lost infrastructure review safeguards.

analysis.notes: AB 2005 amends Government Code Section 66411.7 — the SB 9 urban lot split statute — likely to weaken or eliminate the owner-occupancy condition that currently allows cities to require the applicant to occupy one of the resulting parcels as a primary residence. Removing or restricting that owner-occupancy condition strips a key local gatekeeping tool, enabling investor-driven lot splits that cities cannot discretionarily deny, and extends ministerial by-right approval to a broader class of applicants. The Civil Code addition (Section 1102.6l) likely imposes a disclosure requirement tied to lot split eligibility, further embedding state oversight into what was previously a local land use determination. Cost burden on cities is indirect — increased ministerial processing load without new fee authority.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2005 amends the SB 9 urban lot split framework to curtail or remove the owner-occupancy requirement that cities currently use to limit investor-driven lot splits, mandating ministerial approval for a wider class of applicants regardless of local preference.
• Weakens or eliminates the owner-occupancy condition in Government Code 66411.7, stripping cities of a primary tool to screen out speculative lot splits
• Extends mandatory ministerial (no-discretion) urban lot split approvals to non-owner-occupant applicants, overriding local land use judgment
• Adds a state-mandated Civil Code disclosure requirement, further embedding state authority into a process cities previously controlled
• Increased ministerial processing volume with no new local fee authority to offset administrative costs
Recommended: Oppose — removes the one meaningful local safeguard in the SB 9 lot split process with no replacement mechanism for cities to manage speculative development pressure.

actions.description: Read first time. To print.

analysis.notes: This bill amends the Affordable Housing and High Road Jobs Act of 2022 (Gov. Code §§65912.101, 65912.123), which already establishes a use-by-right approval pathway. Amendments to these sections most likely expand or clarify the ministerial approval mandate and the role of objective standards — both of which directly preempt local discretionary zoning authority, eliminate CEQA and hearing rights for qualifying projects, and compel cities to approve qualifying affordable housing projects regardless of local land use preferences. The infrastructure burden is moderate given ministerial approval pathways that limit cities' ability to condition approvals on utility or frontage improvements.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2118 amends the Affordable Housing and High Road Jobs Act of 2022 to modify use-by-right approval rules and objective standards, further constraining how cities may review and condition qualifying affordable housing projects.
• Expands or entrenches ministerial (no-discretion) approval for qualifying projects, stripping cities of case-by-case zoning and land use authority under §65912.101.
• Limits cities to "objective standards" only under §65912.123, eliminating design review, conditional use permits, and public hearings that communities rely on for neighborhood-sensitive development decisions.
• Forces cities to approve qualifying projects regardless of local general plan or zoning preferences, effectively mandating development volumes and types beyond community choice.
• Ministerial approval pathways constrain cities' ability to require infrastructure improvements or utility upgrades as conditions of approval, shifting potential capacity costs to existing residents.
Recommended: Oppose — bill entrenches state preemption of local planning authority with no meaningful mechanism for communities to ensure projects are compatible with local infrastructure capacity or neighborhood character.

analysis.model: claude-sonnet-4-6 (via Claude Code) — D criterion reanalysis
analysis.comms_brief:
AB2288 forces cities to allow residential conversions in industrial-zoned areas, directly preempting employment land protections that communities have established through local planning.
• Preempts industrial land use designations: cities lose authority over employment zone preservation, undermining economic development strategies and tax base
• Threatens jobs and economic development: converting industrial to residential removes land cities rely on for tax-generating employment — a one-way transition with no reversal
• Infrastructure mismatch: industrial zones lack residential utilities, parks, and pedestrian infrastructure — cities cannot require developers to fund the needed upgrades for livable residential use
• No local environmental review: conversions proceed ministerially even on sites with contamination history or incompatible adjacent operations

analysis.notes: AB 2295 adds Section 65584.2.3 to the Government Code, which relates to the Regional Housing Need Allocation (RHNA) process with a focus on affordable housing. Bills modifying RHNA methodology or allocation formulas partially constrain how cities plan and zone by adjusting the state-imposed housing quotas they must accommodate, representing a moderate local control override and moderate mandate pressure. The bill's early stage and limited digest prevent precise scoring of infrastructure cost impacts, though RHNA-driven compliance obligations routinely generate indirect staffing and planning burdens for localities.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2295 modifies the Regional Housing Need Allocation process to adjust how affordable housing need is calculated and assigned to cities and counties, potentially increasing or reshaping local housing quotas outside of local deliberation.
• RHNA changes constrain local General Plan and zoning choices by setting state-imposed numerical targets cities must plan for, regardless of local infrastructure, capacity, or community preference.
• Revised allocation formulas may increase the share of affordable units cities are required to site, effectively directing land use policy from Sacramento rather than city hall.
• Cities that fail to meet adjusted RHNA targets face builder's remedy exposure and loss of local zoning authority, creating strong compliance pressure even if penalties are not explicit in this bill.
Recommended: Monitor — bill is pre-committee with limited text available; risk level depends heavily on whether the allocation formula increases local quotas or imposes new methodology; full bill text should be reviewed once available.

analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2433 expands California's Density Bonus Law by amending Government Code Section 65915, requiring cities to grant additional density, incentives, and waivers to qualifying projects beyond what local zoning and planning allow.
• Directly preempts local zoning authority by compelling cities to approve density levels, unit counts, or project configurations not permitted under their own general plans or zoning codes.

• Expands mandated concessions and incentives that cities must grant developers, effectively forcing local governments to subsidize project costs through reduced standards without local deliberation.
• Increased density bonus projects approved ministerially or with limited conditions can strain water, sewer, and transportation infrastructure without adequate local authority to require developer-funded mitigation.
Recommended: Oppose — density bonus expansions systematically override local zoning and planning decisions with no opt-out mechanism for communities facing genuine infrastructure or capacity constraints.

AB2702.title: Planning and zoning: streamlined approval: residential care facilities for the elderly.
AB2702.author: Heath Flora
AB2702.summary: An act relating to land use.

actions.description: Read first time. To print.
From printer. May be heard in committee March 23.

analysis.notes: AB 2702 is titled as a streamlined approval bill for residential care facilities for the elderly (RCFEs), a land use category that cities currently regulate through discretionary zoning and permitting. "Streamlined approval" language in California housing law typically means ministerial or by-right approval, which would strip cities of discretionary review authority — including design review, conditional use permits, and CEQA applicability — for qualifying RCFE projects. The infrastructure burden is indirect: ministerial approvals may prevent cities from conditioning RCFE projects on adequate utility or access improvements, though the fiscal impact is uncertain without the full bill text.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2702 would establish a streamlined (likely ministerial, by-right) approval pathway for residential care facilities for the elderly, overriding local discretionary zoning and permitting authority for this use class.
• Preempts local zoning by mandating approval of RCFEs regardless of whether the use is permitted or conditionally permitted under local ordinance
• Eliminates discretionary review tools — design review, conditional use permits, public hearings — that cities use to evaluate neighborhood compatibility and site suitability for care facilities
• Likely triggers CEQA exemption for qualifying projects, removing a key environmental and community review tool
• May prevent cities from imposing infrastructure conditions (traffic, utility, access improvements) as a condition of approval
Recommended: Monitor pending full bill text and committee referral — if "streamlined" is confirmed as ministerial/by-right with CEQA exemption, escalate to Oppose based on removal of all local discretion over a sensitive land use.

Recommended: Oppose/Monitor — bill's multi-section structure touches local discretion, subdivision conditions, and private land use authority simultaneously; full bill text review recommended to confirm scope of each provision.

SB1258.title: Streamlined housing approvals: hazardous waste sites.
SB1258.author: Scott Wiener
SB1258.summary: An act to amend Section 65913.4 of the Government Code, relating to housing.

actions.description: Introduced. Read first time. To Com. on RLS. for assignment. To print.

analysis.notes: SB 1258 amends Government Code §65913.4 — the SB 35 streamlined ministerial approval statute — to expand its applicability to hazardous waste sites, which are currently excluded. This directly preempts local zoning and discretionary review authority by mandating ministerial (by-right) approval for qualifying multifamily projects on sites that cities may have deliberately excluded from residential development pipelines due to contamination concerns. The cost-to-cities risk is moderate: ministerial approval pathways prevent cities from conditioning approvals on full remediation timelines or site-specific infrastructure improvements, potentially shifting liability management and public health oversight costs onto local governments.
analysis.comms_brief:
SB 1258 expands the SB 35 ministerial housing approval mandate to hazardous waste sites, stripping cities of discretionary authority to condition or deny housing projects on contaminated land.
• Eliminates local discretionary review — including public hearings, design review, and conditional use permits — for qualifying projects on hazardous waste sites via ministerial approval mandate
• Preempts cities' ability to apply site-specific remediation conditions or sequencing requirements before housing approvals on contaminated parcels
• Removes cities' authority to pace or stage approvals in coordination with state and federal environmental cleanup timelines, potentially forcing approvals before remediation is complete
• Limits cities' ability to impose infrastructure and public health conditions tied to contamination status, shifting risk management responsibility onto local governments without state funding backstop
Recommended: Oppose — mandating ministerial approval on hazardous waste sites removes the last layer of local oversight protecting residents from premature development on contaminated land, with no mechanism to ensure remediation is complete before occupancy.

analysis.comms_brief:
SB 1353 amends state law governing when alcohol and drug recovery or treatment facilities must be treated as a residential use of property, potentially expanding state preemption of local zoning and limiting local conditions on these facilities.
• Existing §11834.23 already requires cities to classify qualifying facilities as residential by right — amendments could broaden the facilities entitled to this override, reducing local discretion over what facilities operate in residential neighborhoods.
• By mandating residential use classification, the bill limits cities' ability to apply local zoning standards, neighborhood compatibility review, or conditions tailored to community context.
• Any expansion of "by right" residential status further erodes the ability of local governments to evaluate siting, density of similar uses, or proximity concerns through discretionary review.
Recommended: Monitor — the full bill text is not available and the specific amendment language is critical to determining whether this materially expands state preemption beyond current law; fetch full text before taking a formal position.

analysis.comms_brief:
AB 748 mandates that cities and counties establish and administer a preapproved housing plan program, creating a streamlined ministerial-style approval pathway that bypasses normal local discretionary review for qualifying single-family and multifamily projects.
• Partial preemption of local authority: cities must accept and process preapproved prototype plans, limiting their ability to apply locally-adopted design standards or require project-specific review.
• Removes discretionary review: qualifying projects submitted under preapproved plans are processed outside standard public hearing, design review, and conditional use permit processes.
• Mandates development approval: cities are effectively required to approve projects conforming to preapproved plans, reducing community choice over housing type and design in local neighborhoods.
• Unfunded compliance burden: local planning departments must build and maintain a new preapproval administrative infrastructure with no guaranteed state funding offset.
Recommended: Oppose — the bill imposes a mandatory preapproval program that erodes local design authority and generates unfunded administrative costs with no mechanism to recover expenses or preserve meaningful community input.

analysis.notes: SB 677 amends Government Code §65912.156, which sits within California's existing Transit-Oriented Development (TOD) streamlining framework. TOD statutes in this section typically mandate ministerial, by-right approval for qualifying housing projects near transit, overriding local zoning and eliminating discretionary review including CEQA. The amendment likely expands eligibility criteria, removes existing local conditions, or strengthens the ministerial approval mandate — all of which are strong local control overrides. Infrastructure burden is moderate because ministerial approvals under TOD law typically restrict the infrastructure conditions and objective standards cities may impose.
analysis.comms_brief:
SB 677 amends California's transit-oriented development streamlining statute (Gov. Code §65912.156) to expand or strengthen mandatory ministerial approval of housing projects near transit, overriding local zoning, eliminating discretionary review, and limiting cities' ability to impose conditions.
• Explicit state preemption of local zoning: qualifying TOD projects must be approved ministerially regardless of what local plans or community standards allow
• Elimination of CEQA and discretionary review: cities lose the ability to conduct environmental review, hold public hearings, or apply design review to covered projects
• Mandatory development approval: cities are compelled to approve projects that meet state-defined criteria, removing local deliberation over density and housing type
• Infrastructure conditions constrained: ministerial approval pathways limit the infrastructure improvements and impact conditions cities may require, shifting capacity costs to existing residents
Recommended: Oppose — bill strips cities of zoning authority, eliminates public review, and mandates approvals without preserving local infrastructure protection tools; fetch full text recommended to confirm scope of §65912.156 amendment.

analysis.notes: AB 1621 amends both the Housing Accountability Act (HAA, §65589.5) and the postentitlement phase permit statute (§65913.3), which together restrict cities' ability to deny or condition entitled housing projects and require ministerial processing of postentitlement permits (building, grading, encroachment, etc.). Extending or tightening HAA provisions further limits cities' grounds to reject or modify compliant projects, while strengthening §65913.3 removes remaining local discretion over the permit-issuance phase of approved housing developments.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 1621 amends the Housing Accountability Act and postentitlement phase permit law to further constrain cities' authority to deny, condition, or delay housing projects that have already received entitlements.
• Strengthened HAA provisions narrow the grounds on which cities may lawfully disapprove or reduce the density of a compliant housing project, expanding state override of local land use decisions.
• Tightened postentitlement permit rules (§65913.3) push building, grading, and encroachment permits toward ministerial approval, removing remaining city discretion to impose conditions or require corrections during the permit phase.
• Cities that fail to comply face heightened HAA liability — including attorney's fees and court-ordered approvals — creating significant legal and financial exposure that effectively coerces approval of projects cities might otherwise condition.
• Combined amendments compress the window in which local governments can raise infrastructure, design, or public-safety concerns after entitlement is granted.
Recommended: Oppose — dual amendments to HAA and postentitlement law stack local control restrictions with no offsetting mechanism for cities to address infrastructure or public-safety concerns post-entitlement.

analysis.notes: SB 1361 amends Government Code §65912.157, which sits within the Transit-Oriented Development (TOD) streamlining statutes. These provisions typically impose ministerial or by-right approval requirements on housing near transit, limiting local zoning discretion, conditioning approval on objective standards only, restricting CEQA review, and preventing cities from applying local design or density standards that would otherwise block qualifying projects near transit corridors. The amendment likely expands eligible transit project types or local government applicability, broadening the preemptive reach of the TOD regime.
